cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, a U.S. senator and Yale Law founder

narrative_blocks.header: Chaplain of the US Senate
narrative_blocks.detail: Sereno Edwards Dwight served as Chaplain of the Senate of the United States from December 16, 1816 to December 19, 1817. He was ordained at Park Street Church, Boston on September 3, 1817, and served as its pastor 1817-1826 — where he greatly influenced the young hymn writer Ray Palmer, author of "My Faith Looks Up to Thee." As 3rd President of Hamilton College 1833-1835, his career was cut short by accidental mercury poisoning that interfered with his work both in Boston and at Hamilton College, making "his life after 1839 solitary and comparatively uninfluential." He produced the definitive 10-volume Life and Works of Jonathan Edwards (1830) — he was Edwards's great-grandson — which remains a key scholarly edition.
narrative_blocks.body: Served as Chaplain of the United States Senate 1816-1817; ordained at Park Street Church, Boston; served as 3rd President of Hamilton College 1833-1835. His career was cut short by accidental mercury poisoning.

narrative_blocks.header: Chaplain of the US Senate, 1816-1817
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale 1803 + Yale tutor + New Haven lawyer he was licensed to preach in 1816 and chosen Chaplain of the United States Senate that December. He served one Congressional year until December 1817, then was ordained pastor of the Park Street Church Boston where he served nine years.

narrative_blocks.header: Third president of Hamilton College, then mercury poisoned
narrative_blocks.body: He served as the third President of Hamilton College in Clinton NY from 1833 to 1835 — his brother Benjamin Woolsey Dwight was the college's treasurer at the same time. His career was wrecked shortly after by accidental mercury poisoning; the last decade of his life was 'solitary and comparatively uninfluential.'

narrative_blocks.header: Ten-volume Jonathan Edwards edition still a primary source
narrative_blocks.body: As a great-grandson of Jonathan Edwards, he published the Life and Works of Jonathan Edwards in ten volumes in 1830 — still consulted as a key primary source on the Northampton theologian. He also wrote the Life of David Brainerd (1822) and The Hebrew Wife (1836).

narrative_blocks.header: First sermon at Center Church, father's last
narrative_blocks.body: Licensed to preach October 1816, he delivered his first sermon at Center Church New Haven; that same afternoon in the same pulpit his father Pope Dwight preached what proved to be his last sermon. Six weeks later he was elected Chaplain of the U.S. Senate; the next year Lyman Beecher preached his ordination at Park Street Church Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: Only child died on the day of birth
narrative_blocks.body: In August 1811 he married Susan Edwards Daggett, second daughter of CT Chief Justice David Daggett. Their only child died on the day of its birth; Susan herself, weakened by chronic dyspepsia, died in 1839 at her father David Daggett's New Haven house in her 52nd year, leaving Sereno childless and increasingly solitary for eleven more years.

artworks.blurb: Portrait by John Vanderlyn at Hamilton College President's House — given to college 1984 by Col. Dwight Garrison USAR.

artworks.blurb: Portrait by John Vanderlyn at Hamilton College President's House — given to college 1984 by Col. Dwight Garrison USAR.

parents.mother_name: Wife of David Daggett (first name pending research)

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with husband Rev. Sereno Edwards Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: Chief Justice's daughter married Pope Dwight's son
narrative_blocks.body: Her father David Daggett served as Chief Justice of Connecticut and US Senator from Connecticut; her husband Rev. Sereno Edwards Dwight was a son of Pope of Yale Timothy Dwight IV. The 1811 marriage joined two of New Haven's leading Federalist families in one household.

cross_connections.notes: No other family has provided three presidents of Yale. Timothy Dwight College at Yale is named for both the 8th and 12th presidents.
No other family has provided three presidents of Yale. Timothy Dwight College at Yale is named for both the 8th and 12th presidents.

cross_connections.display_label: Called Yale colleague James Luce Kingsley 'the American Addison'

cross_connections.description: Talcott-side descendant given middle name 'Dwight' in 1855, likely after Pope Dwight (8th Yale president); a second Timothy Dwight Hobart (TD0131 b.1826) carries the same name pattern.

narrative_blocks.header: He made Yale the nation’s largest college
narrative_blocks.body: As Yale’s eighth president from 1795 to 1817 he hired Benjamin Silliman as its first chemistry professor — later called the patriarch of American science — and grew Yale into the largest college in the country. His revival sermons there helped touch off the Second Great Awakening.

narrative_blocks.header: He argued for independence a year early
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1775, a full year before the Declaration, he was openly urging the colonies to declare independence — by his own account using the very arguments later adopted — while many around him were still fearful or opposed.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote America’s first epic poem
narrative_blocks.body: In 1785 he published “The Conquest of Canaan,” an eleven-book biblical epic dedicated to George Washington and reckoned the first epic poem written in America. He was a leading “Hartford Wit” alongside John Trumbull, Joel Barlow, and Noah Webster.

narrative_blocks.header: A mission in Arkansas still bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: He helped found the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions in 1810; a decade later, in 1820, its Dwight Mission to the Cherokee in Arkansas was named in his honor. As a Continental Army chaplain in the Revolution he had written the patriotic song “Columbia” for his troops.

narrative_blocks.header: Stolen pears taught him the eighth commandment
narrative_blocks.body: As a small boy he took pears from a neighbor’s yard with older schoolmates; his mother told him it broke the commandment against stealing. He returned them in tears and refused to keep them even when the owner offered — a story he later preached to his Yale congregation.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote a war anthem as army chaplain
narrative_blocks.body: While serving as a chaplain in the Continental Army, Timothy Dwight wrote the patriotic song "Columbia." Set to music, it became one of the young republic's stirring anthems.

artworks.blurb: John Trumbull oil portrait, painted 1817 — year of Pope Dwight's death.

institutions.institution_blurb: Founding figure; Dwight Mission named in his honor.

institutions.blurb: Taught at Hopkins Grammar School in New Haven after his 1769 Yale graduation, before becoming a Yale Tutor.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with wife Mary Woolsey, son James Dwight III, granddaughter Aurelia, grandson Tim V

education.notes: Entered Yale at age 13; graduated top of

career.notes: pastor; ran a noted boys’ academy

parents.father_name: Benjamin Woolsey of Long Island NY — was Maj. Timothy Dwight III's Yale classmate, roommate, and intimate friend
parents.father_research_notes: Mary's father Benjamin Woolsey predeceased the Dwight marriage. Earlier tree data incorrectly set father_id=H00914 (her HUSBAND Pope Dwight). Corrected Stage 6Y-161.

bio.extra_names: Sometimes Margaret (EH 914 calls her Margaret; FAG and consensus sources Mary; per Sam, the same person — Mary as nickname for Margaret)

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with husband Pope Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: Brother and sister-in-law cemented Woolsey-Dwight ties
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Benjamin Woolsey had been Maj. Timothy Dwight III's Yale classmate, roommate, and intimate friend — the Woolsey-Dwight connection preceded her own 1777 marriage to Pope Dwight. Her brother William Walter Woolsey later married Elizabeth Dwight, sister of her husband Pope Dwight, doubling the family link in the next generation.

burial.plot_notes: Unknown grave near Natchez, Adams County, Mississippi — died in the wilderness 1777, body location never recorded

career.notes: Merchant in Northampton MA. Substantial family business.
Judge 1758-1774. Took oath of fealty to British crown on assuming office — held this oath binding through the Revolution and refused to revoke it.

narrative_blocks.header: Loyalist judge who would not break his oath
narrative_blocks.body: He had sworn fealty to the British crown in 1758 on becoming judge of the court of common pleas, and held that oath binding when the Revolution came. His Northampton family suffered increasing community ostracism as the war approached; one biographer called him 'a Loyalist on Christian principle, and yet thoroughly patriotic in his feelings.'

narrative_blocks.header: Took two sons to a Mississippi colony, 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Disillusioned with revolutionary Northampton, in May 1776 he sailed from Middletown CT with two of his sons — Sereno and Jonathan — toward a British land grant at Natchez Mississippi. They were accompanied by the Phelps and Lyman families and the Rev Mr Smith's household; within a year he and Mrs Lyman would both be dead at Natchez of fever.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in the wilderness, body never recovered
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Natchez Mississippi in June 1777 at age fifty in the wilderness, without trial or recorded grave. News of his death traveled slowly back to Northampton; his son Timothy Dwight IV left the Continental Army in 1778 to return home as the oldest surviving male and take charge of the family's affairs.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried tiny wife on his palm, arm's length
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Mary Edwards Dwight was as small as he was large — he sometimes carried her around the room on his open palm held out at arm's length. She bore him thirteen children, eight of them sons, all noted for fine physical forms — the smallest son Cecil was five-foot-eight-and-a-half and weighed two hundred pounds.

cross_connections.display_label: Refused his Northampton congregation, took communion twelve miles away instead.

narrative_blocks.header: Married at sixteen as Edwards left for Stockbridge
narrative_blocks.body: Her November 1750 wedding to Major Timothy Dwight came two months before Jonathan Edwards moved his family to the Stockbridge mission. The marriage was permitted because the family was about to be separated.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Yale's eighth president Timothy Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Timothy Dwight IV led Yale College from 1795 to 1817. He shaped a generation of New England intellectual life from his grandfather Edwards's old territory.

narrative_blocks.header: Refused her pew when Hooker succeeded her father
narrative_blocks.body: When the distant Thomas Hooker descendant Rev. John Hooker became Northampton's pastor after Edwards's dismissal, she placed her own chair in the vestibule below the belfry. On communion Sabbaths she rode twelve miles on a pillion behind her son to take communion at another church.

narrative_blocks.header: Heard a year later her husband had died
narrative_blocks.body: Major Timothy Dwight III died at Natchez, Mississippi in 1777 during a southern trip. The news did not reach her at Northampton until a full year after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband could carry her on his open palm
narrative_blocks.body: She was unusually small in stature and was described in old age as 'opinionated and self-willed.' Patriot rabble burned her fields and stole her livestock during the Revolution because of her husband's Tory sympathies.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST027.name: Hamilton College
INST027.notes: Liberal arts college in Clinton, Oneida County NY. Sereno Edwards Dwight (HD1970) served as 3rd President 1833-1835 (his career cut short by mercury poisoning). Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight (HD1988) graduated 1835; was tutor 1839-1842. Theodore William Dwight (HD1989) graduated 1840; taught 1842-1858; Dean of Hamilton Law School 1853 before moving to Columbia. Elliott Anthony (husband of HD1991) studied law here under Theodore William Dwight. Benjamin Woolsey Dwight (HD1967) was treasurer of Hamilton College. Asahel Clark Kendrick (I00943) graduated A.B. 1831 and immediately joined the faculty as a tutor, beginning his career in classical languages before moving to Madison University (Colgate).

INST032.name: Park Street Church, Boston
INST032.notes: Sereno Edwards Dwight (HD1970), son of Yale president Timothy Dwight IV (H00914), was ordained here Sep 3, 1817, and served as pastor 1817-1826. During his pastorate he greatly influenced young hymn writer Ray Palmer (1808-1887), author of "My Faith Looks Up to Thee." Tapping Reeve (X00614, founder of Litchfield Law School) is also connected: Dwight brought Lyman Beecher to Litchfield in 1810 to serve as Presbyterian minister, and Beecher later served at Park Street Church.
